stories.original:
**FCA sets out open finance roadmap tied to smart data legislation**

*The FCA has published a delivery plan for open finance, linking its approach to the government's broader smart data framework and signalling a phased expansion beyond payments accounts.*

The roadmap, outlined by the FCA and analysed by Lewis Silkin, commits the regulator to enabling open finance development as a stated priority within its 2025–2030 strategy. The plan envisions extending consent-based data sharing to savings, investments, pensions and insurance, building on the existing open banking infrastructure rather than replacing it.

The timing matters because it aligns with the UK government's Smart Data legislation, which provides the statutory basis for cross-sector data sharing. The FCA's roadmap effectively positions open finance as one application of that broader framework, rather than a standalone regulatory initiative. For firms already operating in open banking, this confirms the direction of travel: the data-sharing model will widen, but within a structure that prioritises interoperability with existing smart data provisions.

The practical question remains sequencing. The FCA has committed to a delivery plan but the roadmap does not yet specify binding timelines for individual asset classes. Firms building open finance propositions will need to track the FCA's follow-up consultations closely.

*Editorial note: This is the most significant European regulatory development in today's feed, confirming the FCA's concrete commitment to open finance and connecting it to the smart data legislative programme.*

*Sources: [FCA issues open finance roadmap with vision for smart data future](https://www.lewissilkin.com/insights/2026/05/12/fca-issues-open-finance-roadmap-with-vision-for-smart-data-future-102msah) — Lewis Silkin | [FCA issues open finance roadmap with vision for smart data future](https://www.lexology.com/library/detail.aspx?g=13e98593-dc6f-4b4c-b6af-bbc43712e7a6) — Lexology*

stories.original:
**King's Speech confirms PSR will merge into the FCA**

*The UK government has formally confirmed its intention to fold the Payment Systems Regulator into the Financial Conduct Authority, settling months of speculation about the future of payments oversight.*

The announcement came as part of the King's Speech setting out the government's legislative agenda for the new parliamentary session. The merger, alongside reforms to the Financial Ombudsman Service, signals a consolidation of financial regulatory architecture that the government has framed as a simplification measure.

For payments firms, the practical implications are significant. The PSR has operated as the specialist regulator for payment systems since 2015, overseeing interchange fee compliance, access to payment systems and, more recently, APP fraud reimbursement rules. Absorbing it into the FCA raises questions about whether payments will retain dedicated regulatory attention or be subsumed into the FCA's broader supervisory agenda. The transition timeline and the fate of ongoing PSR workstreams, such as the new reimbursement framework, will be critical to watch as the legislation progresses.

*Editorial note: The formal confirmation of the PSR–FCA merger in the King's Speech moves this from policy rumour to legislative commitment, with direct consequences for every regulated payments firm in the UK.*

*Sources: [King Charles III Outlines Government Legislative Agenda for New Session](https://world.infonasional.com/king-speech-government-legislative-agenda) — InfoNasional*

stories.original:
**Moneyline adopts NatWest Payit for variable recurring payments on loans**

*Community lender Moneyline has selected NatWest's Payit platform to offer variable recurring payments for loan repayments, extending VRP beyond its initial subscription and sweeping use cases into consumer credit.*

Moneyline, which serves borrowers on low incomes across the UK, will use VRP to give customers more flexible, borrower-controlled repayment options as an alternative to direct debits. The integration with NatWest's Payit platform makes this one of the more visible deployments of VRP in a lending context.

The significance lies in the use case rather than the deal size. VRP adoption has so far been concentrated in sweeping between a customer's own accounts and in subscription billing. Applying it to loan repayments tests whether the consent-based, variable-amount model can work in credit, where payment amounts may fluctuate and where borrower control over timing could reduce missed payments and associated fees. If this model proves effective for a community lender's customer base, it strengthens the case for VRP as a genuine direct debit alternative in regulated credit markets.

*Editorial note: This is a meaningful proof point for VRP's expansion into lending, a segment where the direct debit replacement thesis faces its most practical test.*

*Sources: [Moneyline selects NatWest Payit for variable recurring payments](https://www.finextra.com/pressarticle/109803/moneyline-selects-natwest-payit-for-variable-recurring-payments) — Finextra | [NatWest makes 'huge' change for customers with Direct Debits](https://www.birminghammail.co.uk/news/cost-of-living/natwest-makes-huge-change-customers-33932394) — Birmingham Live*

stories.original:
**Oxford academic paper positions FiDA as open banking's extension into capital markets**

*A new analysis from the Oxford Business Law Blog frames the EU's proposed Financial Data Access regulation as a mechanism to apply open banking principles to investor data, linking it to capital markets integration goals.*

The paper, published by the University of Oxford, examines how FiDA would extend consent-based data sharing beyond payment accounts into investment products. It argues that standardised investor data portability could reduce information asymmetries, improve investor protection and support the EU's capital markets union objectives.

For open banking practitioners, the analysis is useful because it maps FiDA's scope against the existing PSD2/PSD3 framework and identifies where the regulation creates genuinely new data-sharing obligations rather than simply extending existing ones. The paper highlights that FiDA's ambition to cover savings, investments, pensions and insurance products means the technical and governance challenges will be materially different from those encountered in payments. Firms positioning for open finance in the EU should note that the academic and policy consensus is converging on FiDA as the vehicle, with investor data now explicitly in scope alongside the more commonly discussed retail finance categories.

*Editorial note: This Oxford analysis adds substantive depth to the FiDA discussion, connecting it to capital markets policy in a way that is directly relevant to firms planning their open finance strategy in the EU.*

*Sources: [Investor Data Sharing in the EU: Enhancing Investor Protection and Capital Market Integration](https://blogs.law.ox.ac.uk/oblb/blog-post/2026/05/investor-data-sharing-eu-enhancing-investor-protection-and-capital-market) — Oxford Business Law Blog*

---
